Workshop

Build Your Own
AI Agents

From LLMs to Autonomous Systems
AMD GPUs  ·  Open-Weight Models  ·  OpenClaw

Mahdi Ghodsi
Eda Zhou
Part 1

Foundations

What is an LLM?

A model trained on a large amount of text. Given a prompt, it predicts the next tokens and produces a response. By itself, it is a text-generation system.

What it can do

  • Answer questions
  • Summarize, translate, classify text
  • Generate and explain code
  • Transform or rewrite content

What it cannot do by itself

  • Act on external systems
  • Maintain persistent memory
  • Manage a multi-step process
  • Verify or correct its own output
On its own, an LLM generates a response. It does not independently plan, use tools, or complete tasks.

LLM → Chatbot → Agent

Each step adds a capability. The jump from chatbot to agent is the biggest one.

LLM
Answers questions based on training data Every response is stateless. It has no memory of what came before.
Chatbot
LLM + the conversation history injected as context Feels like it remembers (it is just reading the transcript each time)
Agent
LLM + memory + planning + ability to take actions in the world Can run code, call APIs, read files, send messages, and loop until the task is done

How Agents Think: The ReAct Loop

Thoughtreason about
what to do next
→
Actioncall a tool
→
Observationread the result
back into context
⟳

↩ loop repeats until the task is complete

ThoughtThe model decides what the next step should be based on everything it knows so far
ActionIt calls a tool (run code, search the web, read a file, send a message)
ObservationThe tool result comes back and the model reads it, then loops again

Why the Loop Changes Everything

Without a loop

  • Ask the model to fix a bug
  • It proposes a fix
  • You still don't know whether it works

With a loop

  • Read the file
  • Run the tests, see the error
  • Edit the code
  • Run the tests again
  • Stop when verified ✓
In practice, execution, checking, and retrying are what make agents reliable. A stronger loop often matters more than a stronger model.
Part 2

The Recipe

The Three Components of an Agent

An agent is not just a model. It is a model inside a system with three distinct parts.

① Model
The reasoning and language engine: decides what to do next
② Runtime
Manages the loop, context, tool calls, retries, and state
③ Tools
The external capabilities the agent can call: files, APIs, code execution, messaging

Component 1: The Model

For agent work, what matters is reliable tool calling, a long context window, and the ability to follow multi-step instructions without drifting. These open-source models all hit that bar today:

  • Gemma 4 (Google): multimodal, native function calling, 256K context, day-0 AMD support via vLLM and SGLang
  • Qwen3.5 (Alibaba): 256K context, strong MCP and coding tool use
  • Kimi K2.5 (Moonshot AI): MoE, 32B active parameters, long-horizon tasks
  • MiniMax M2.5 (MiniMax): deep context retention
  • GPT-OSS (OpenAI): open-weight reasoning, fits a single H100 or MI300X
All run on AMD MI300X. All support structured tool calls. The right choice depends on your task, context requirements, and hardware.

Component 2: The Agent Runtime

The runtime receives the prompt and context, catches the model's tool call, executes it, applies guardrails, and feeds the result back into the next turn.

send prompt  →  parse tool calls  →  run tools  →  append results  →  repeat

Concrete runtime jobs

  • Prompt assembly: combines instructions, memory, chat history, and tool definitions
  • Tool execution: catches JSON tool calls and runs them against the real system
  • Guardrails and recovery: approvals, retries, timeouts, fallbacks, safety controls
  • Integration layer: connects custom tools, APIs, CLIs, and MCP servers

Component 3: Tools

A tool only becomes useful once it is described in a way the model can call: a name, a description, and a typed schema. The model sees that interface. It does not see the implementation.

Custom tools you define

  • A typed function with a name and description
  • A parameter schema the model uses to call it
  • Exposed to the model in its tool format at session start
  • Examples: read file, send message, run query, create ticket

External tools you connect

  • MCP servers: GitHub, Slack, Postgres, Stripe, Notion, Gmail
  • APIs: any REST/GraphQL endpoint wrapped as a tool
  • CLIs: any installed program via shell exec
  • Plugins: packaged tool sets with auth and lifecycle
No matter where the capability comes from, the agent only sees a structured tool interface.
Part 3

The Framework Explosion

The Framework Explosion

There was a large explosion of agent frameworks. Most of them were solving the same recurring engineering problems.

① Loop orchestrationSend → parse → run → append → repeat
② Context managementConversations grow until they exceed the model's memory
③ Error recoveryTools fail, APIs error, models timeout
④ Observability and evaluationWhat did the agent do? Did it work? How do you trace a failure?

Different Frameworks, Same Loop

Each framework exposes the ReAct loop through a different abstraction. The primitives underneath are the same.

FrameworkAbstraction style
LangChain / LangGraphChain and graph orchestration: wire steps together declaratively
CrewAI / AutoGenMulti-agent coordination: assign roles, run parallel agents
LlamaIndexData and retrieval workflows: connectors and retrieval pipelines built in
SmolagentsCode-driven: agents write Python instead of JSON tool calls
Frameworks are a good default when you want to move fast. Building your own runtime is still a valid choice when you need precise control over execution, observability, or integration behavior.
Part 4

The OpenClaw Approach

Not a Framework: a Runtime

Most agent frameworks are libraries: you import them, write the loop, and run a script. OpenClaw is a different model.

Framework approach

  • A library you import into your code
  • You write the loop, routing, and error handling
  • Runs when your script runs, stops when it ends
  • One session at a time by default
  • Reactive: waits for your code to call it

OpenClaw approach

  • A persistent process running on your machine
  • It owns the loop, error handling, and routing
  • Always running: messages, cron jobs, webhooks
  • Multi-session, multi-channel, concurrent by default
  • Proactive: time and events are first-class inputs
The persistent-process model is what makes lanes, compaction, cron, self-restart, and multi-channel routing possible without glue code.

What Happens When You Send a Message

OpenClaw is an operating system for AI agents. The model provides intelligence. The gateway handles everything else, on hardware you control.

① Message arrives
From Slack, Discord, Telegram, web, iMessage, or any connected channel
↓
② Session lane
Queued per conversation. One turn at a time. No race conditions across concurrent users.
↓
③ Prompt assembled
Bootstrap files, tool schemas, and conversation history combined
↓
④ Model generates
Text reply → delivered. Tool call → executed, result appended, loop continues.
↓
⑤ Reply delivered
Back to the originating channel. The whole stack runs on your own machine.

Proactive by Design

Most AI assistants are reactive: wait for a message, respond, stop. OpenClaw treats time and events as inputs too.

What can trigger the agent

  • A message from any connected channel
  • A cron job: "every morning at 08:00, summarize my calendar"
  • A heartbeat: timer fires, agent decides if anything needs doing
  • A hook: webhook, file change, or API call wakes the agent
  • Another agent: a parent or peer session sends a task

What this changes

  • The agent can initiate work without being asked
  • Long-running workflows survive restarts
  • Monitoring and reminders are first-class
  • The heartbeat is the simplest form: fire a timer, act only if needed

How the Agent Wakes Up

Every session starts with a richly structured system prompt assembled at runtime, not hardcoded. This is how the agent knows its environment before it does anything.

What goes into the prompt

  • Tools section: every tool available this session, with schemas
  • Skills catalog: names & descriptions only; full content loaded on demand
  • Bootstrap files: injected verbatim from your workspace:
    • AGENTS.md: instructions & architectural rules
    • SOUL.md: the agent's personality & identity
    • TOOLS.md: local environment: hostnames, scripts, cameras
    • MEMORY.md: notes the agent wrote in previous sessions
    • USER.md: information about you

Runtime section (injected last)

  • Current date and time
  • Host OS and Node version
  • Active model name and thinking level
  • Session ID

Why this matters

  • The agent doesn't need to ask basic questions; it already knows its workspace, tools, and context
  • Edit a markdown file → agent's behavior changes on next session. No code.
  • Everything is assembled in deterministic order → prompt cache hits every turn

Concurrency Without Chaos

Multiple users, channels, and spawned subagents all run at once. OpenClaw gives every conversation its own serialized queue, and keeps heavy global work on a separate lane.

Session A   ──► [turn 1] ──► [turn 2] ──► [turn 3]
Session B   ──► [turn 1] ──► [turn 2]
Cron       ──► [scheduled job]
Global      ──► [compaction]
Session laneOne turn per session at a time. A slow session never blocks anyone else.
Cron and subagent lanesScheduled jobs and spawned subagents each have their own concurrency caps, configurable independently of the main agent.

Connect Anything: MCP & Plugins

OpenClaw is built to be extended. Two mechanisms let you add capabilities without touching core code.

MCP: Model Context Protocol

An open standard for exposing tools to language models. Configure an MCP server and its full tool catalog appears in the agent's tool list automatically.

  • GitHub, Postgres, Slack, Stripe, Notion. MCP servers exist for all of these
  • Local (subprocess) or remote (HTTP), same config, same interface
  • The agent can't tell the difference between an MCP tool and a native tool
  • No code changes: just add a server to your config

Plugin System

Plugins are the primary extension mechanism. Each plugin declares its capabilities in a manifest and registers hooks; core never needs to know about specific providers.

  • Model providers: add any LLM: Anthropic, Ollama, OpenRouter, Together
  • Channels: add new messaging surfaces (Matrix, Zalo, Voice)
  • Tools & skills: ship new tools or task-specific SKILL.md files
  • Third-party plugins install via npm, no fork required

The Agent Knows Itself

BOOT.md: startup as a task

  • Drop a BOOT.md in your workspace. The agent reads it on first start and acts on it.
  • "Check if the camera is reachable, then ping me." No code, just markdown.

Skills load on demand

  • System prompt lists skill names only, not content.
  • Model picks the right skill and loads it. Catalog can be huge.

Context vs Memory

  • Context: temporary token window. Fills up, gets compacted, continues.
  • Memory: plain files on disk. Survives restarts. Agent reads and writes them.

The Agent Can Restart Its Own Gateway

The agent has a gateway tool. One of its actions is restart. Here is exactly what happens when it calls it:

agent calls gateway(action=restart, reason="updated config")
  → restart sentinel written to disk (captures channel + thread ID)
  → SIGUSR1 sent to gateway process
  → gateway marks itself draining: new requests fail fast
  → in-flight sessions allowed to finish (up to 5 min)
  → OS-level restart
  → sentinel consumed: confirmation routed back to original channel
  → queued work resumes; BOOT.md runs if present
In-flight sessions surviveDraining waits for active turns to finish before the process exits. No work is dropped mid-execution.
Channel routing preservedThe sentinel captures the session key, channel, and thread ID. The restart confirmation lands in the right place.
Transcript always intactThe session JSONL file is never deleted. The full history of every turn and tool call survives the restart on disk.
Part 5

Hands-On: AMD GPU Tutorial

Why AMD GPUs?

AMD Instinct MI300X

  • 192 GB HBM3 unified memory
  • 5.3 TB/s memory bandwidth
  • 304 compute units
  • ROCm open software stack
192 GB of unified memory is enough headroom for large open-weight agent models that would not fit on any single consumer card.

What fits on one card

  • Qwen3.5-122B-FP8 ✓

Software stack

  • ROCm: open GPU compute stack
  • Triton kernels run on ROCm without modification
  • SGLang and vLLM both support MI300X natively

AMD Developer Program

Scan to join

  • $100 AMD developer cloud credits
  • 1 month DeepLearning.AI Pro membership
  • Monthly AMD hardware sweepstakes

Serving Open-Source Models on AMD GPUs

Both SGLang and vLLM expose an OpenAI-compatible HTTP API. OpenClaw points at that URL and the integration is identical either way.

  • Popular, production-ready inference servers with large communities
  • Triton kernels run on ROCm out of the box, no modification needed
  • Both have native AMD MI300X support and active ROCm testing
  • Native tool-call parser flags for agent workflows
  • Extensive documentation and ecosystem resources
Choose whichever your team already knows. From OpenClaw's perspective, they are interchangeable.

The Model: Qwen3.5-122B-FP8

Alibaba's latest, released with AMD day-0 support on MI300X, MI325X, and MI35X.

PropertyValue
ArchitectureMoE + Hybrid Attention
Context window256K tokens
QuantizationFP8, fits one MI300X
LicenseApache 2.0
AMD supportROCm · Triton · day-0

Hybrid Attention Innovation

Gated Delta Networks switch between:

  • Full attention: for associative recall tasks
  • Linear attention: for long-range context at reduced compute

Both implemented as Triton kernels → run on ROCm without modification.

Tutorial: Launch SGLang + Connect OpenClaw

  1. 1
    AMD Developer Cloud → Quick Start → SGLang image → MI300X → launch
  2. 2
    Pull the Docker image:
    docker pull lmsysorg/sglang:v0.5.9-rocm720-mi30x
  3. 3
    Start the server with these key flags:
    --model-path Qwen/Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-FP8
    --attention-backend triton
    --tool-call-parser qwen3_coder
    --reasoning-parser qwen3
  4. 4
    Install OpenClaw → during onboarding enter:
    Base URL: http://<vm-ip>:8090/v1   Model: qwen3-5-122b
  5. 5
    OpenClaw connects. You now have a 122B model with 256K context window serving agent tool calls, running on MI300X hardware via a standard OpenAI-compatible API.

What Just Happened

Map this back to the three components from earlier:

Model
Qwen3.5-122B-FP8: emits structured tool calls via --tool-call-parser qwen3_coder
Runtime
OpenClaw assembles context, catches tool calls, executes them safely, and loops until the task is done
Tools
Custom tools · MCP servers · APIs · CLIs · all filtered through the tool policy pipeline
Inference server
SGLang serves the model as an OpenAI-compatible API on port 8090
Hardware
One AMD MI300X, 192 GB HBM3, enough to fit the full 122B model in memory
Each layer is modular because the interfaces are standardized. Swap any component without touching the others.
Part 6

The Full Stack

The Complete Picture

User
Message from any surface
↓
Channel
Slack · Discord · Telegram · Web · Signal · iMessage
↓
OpenClaw Gateway
Routes to agent · manages sessions · delivers replies
↓
Agent Runtime
ReAct loop · lane-based · tool policy · compaction
SGLang / AMD MI300X
Qwen3.5-122B-FP8 · 256K ctx · tool calling
Tools
exec · web · MCP · subagents · cron · memory

Key Takeaways

  • An LLM generates text from a prompt. It does not act, plan, or maintain state on its own.
  • A chatbot adds conversation state. An agent adds tools, a control loop, and the ability to act.
  • The ReAct pattern (Thought → Action → Observation) is the core loop behind most agents.
  • Building useful agents requires three things: a capable model, a solid runtime, and the right tools.
  • Most frameworks wrap the same core loop with different abstractions. They differ in ergonomics, not fundamentals.
  • Open-weight models on AMD MI300X now match frontier quality. 192 GB fits models no consumer GPU can run.
  • The full stack (AMD GPU + SGLang + OpenClaw) is available to try for free with $100 in AMD credits.
